title: Porcupines and Hedgehogs
wikitext:
[]: Porcupines and Hedgehogs             Quote Post by nomadimp » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:35 am As many people have said "Hedgehog's Dilemma" originates in Schopenhauer's Parerga and Paralipomena . However (in the translation's I find) it is a group of Porcupines in question. Apparently, Freud liked the fable and quoted it (where exactly he did so I have been unable to locate - help here would be welcome), but even in this form, the rodents were Porcupines -- not Hedgehogs. Ive seen it said that "Hedgehog's Dilemma" is a psychological term, but it is not one I have been able to find a single instance of in any psychological journal. All the same, it is most likely that Anno's contact with the concept would be through reading about psychology. So my question is: how did it get from procupines to hedgehogs?

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE 08 » Wed Jan 25, 2006 5:09 am I'm not sure, but Hedgehogs are cuter (and less harmful) than hedgehogs. Recent variations in the US have porcupines in the title as well, I used to have a book of the fable as a kid.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jan 25, 2006 7:48 am Porcupines and New World fauna, hedgehogs Old World. I would expect it to be a translation to a European mammal with the appropriate characteristics.

[]:             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Wed Jan 25, 2006 9:04 am Mr. Tines wrote: Porcupines and New World fauna, hedgehogs Old World. I would expect it to be a translation to a European mammal with the appropriate characteristics.

[]:             Quote Post by Space Penis » Wed Jan 25, 2006 10:29 am Does it really matter whether it's porcupines or hedgehogs? Both are mammals with spikey bits; both can fill the role in the metaphor thingy.

[]:             Quote Post by nomadimp » Wed Jan 25, 2006 1:07 pm I was just curious as to whether or not it really came from Schopenhauer's porcupines or some other hedgehogs that I didnt know about. Thanks for the replies though everyone.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Jan 25, 2006 2:59 pm I never heard of this dilemma until I watched Evangelion. And until just now, I thought it was something Anno made up himself.

[]:             Quote Post by nomadimp » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:08 pm Well, as far as I can tell, Anno made up the term "Hedgehog's Dilemma" - at least I cannot find anyone else referring to it in those terms. The story, however, seems to have originated in Schopenhauer. While I doubt that Anno was any great reader of Schopenhauer, it is interesting to me that his philosophy seems to have indirectly influenced NGE.

[]:             Quote Post by poetic_assassin » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:22 pm Porcupines make more sence anyway. They have longer quill's. Where as, porcupines have those sharp but stuby ones.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:39 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I never heard of this dilemma until I watched Evangelion.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE 08 » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:45 pm My friend used to have a pet hedgehog, they are much smaller, they fit right into the palm of your hand, cutest thing I ever saw. I still sleep with a stuffed hedgehog named Tommy (named after former Sec. of Homeland Security Tom Ridge, yeah I was bored).

[]:             Quote Post by nomadimp » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:46 pm Actually I just read in the commentary project that Reichu: Funny, I thought that was said of porcupines? Same difference, I suppose. Speaking of whichâ€¦ In Japanese, Ritsuko speaks of the "yama'arashi no JIREMA", translated as "hedgehog's dilemma". However, it had been my understanding that the Japanese word for "hedgehog" was harinezumi â€” indeed, this is what our old buddy Sonic is identified as â€” whereas yama'arashi referred to porcupines. So it would seem that "Hedgehog" is simply a result of a mistranslation. Possibly because Japanese tend to prefer the English (British) versions of words to the American. And as was pointed out by Mr. Tines, the English have Hedgehogs, not Porcupines.

[]:             Quote Post by BLACKANGEL32076 » Wed Feb 01, 2006 3:21 pm Could've been a matter of Anno writing Hedgehogs but meaning Porcupines. I guess the spines threw him off.

[]:             Quote Post by TheBlueTree » Wed Feb 01, 2006 4:57 pm Space Penis wrote: Does it really matter whether it's porcupines or hedgehogs? Both are mammals with spikey bits; both can fill the role in the metaphor thingy.

[]: Porcupines and Hedgehogs             Quote Post by nomadimp » Wed Jan 25, 2006 4:35 am As many people have said "Hedgehog's Dilemma" originates in Schopenhauer's Parerga and Paralipomena . However (in the translation's I find) it is a group of Porcupines in question. Apparently, Freud liked the fable and quoted it (where exactly he did so I have been unable to locate - help here would be welcome), but even in this form, the rodents were Porcupines -- not Hedgehogs. Ive seen it said that "Hedgehog's Dilemma" is a psychological term, but it is not one I have been able to find a single instance of in any psychological journal. All the same, it is most likely that Anno's contact with the concept would be through reading about psychology. So my question is: how did it get from procupines to hedgehogs?

Article: Porcupines and Hedgehogs
 the commentary project that Reichu: Funny, I thought that was said of porcupines? Same difference, I suppose. Speaking of whichâ€¦ In Japanese, Ritsuko speaks of the "yama'arashi no JIREMA", translated as "hedgehog's dilemma". However, it had been my understanding that the Japanese word for "hedgehog" was harinezumi â€” indeed, this is what our old buddy Sonic is identified as â€” whereas yama'arashi referred to porcupines. So it would seem that "Hedgehog" is simply a result of a mistranslation. Possibly because Japanese tend to prefer the English (British) versions of words to the American. And as was pointed out by Mr. Tines, the English have Hedgehogs, not Porcupines.
